meta.title: 1.3 What You See on Screen
meta.description: Learn to read Claude Code's visual cues — tool calls, permission prompts, and the status line.

blocks.prompt: When Claude Code is working on a task, what do you think you see in the terminal? Just text appearing, or something more? If you have used Claude Code, what do the colored boxes and prompts mean?

blocks.content:
## Reading Claude's body language

A human colleague gives you signals while working: they pause to think, they ask clarifying questions, they show you drafts. Claude Code does the same thing — through its terminal interface. Learning to read these signals makes you a better collaborator.

### Tool calls: the visible loop

When Claude Code takes an action, you see a **tool call** appear in your terminal. Each tool call has a name and shows what Claude is doing:

| Tool call | What it means | Loop phase |
| :-- | :-- | :-- |
| `Read` | Claude is reading a file | Gather |
| `Glob` | Claude is searching for files by name pattern | Gather |
| `Grep` | Claude is searching file contents for a pattern | Gather |
| `Edit` | Claude is modifying a file | Act |
| `Write` | Claude is creating a new file | Act |
| `Bash` | Claude is running a terminal command | Gather, Act, or Verify |

Watching tool calls is like watching over a colleague's shoulder. You can see *exactly* what Claude is doing at each step.

### Permission prompts: the safety gate

Before Claude takes certain actions, it asks for your permission. You will see a prompt that describes what Claude wants to do and asks you to approve or deny it.

This is your **safety gate**. Claude Code can read files, edit documents, and run commands on your actual computer. Permission prompts ensure you stay in control.

Common responses to permission prompts:
- **Allow once** — Let Claude do this specific action
- **Allow always** — Trust this type of action for the rest of the session
- **Deny** — Block this action

### The status line

At the bottom of your terminal, the status line shows:
- The current **permission mode** (how much trust you are giving Claude)
- The **model** being used
- Token usage and cost information

### Keyboard shortcuts you should know now

| Shortcut | What it does |
| :-- | :-- |
| `Esc` | Stop Claude mid-action |
| `Shift+Tab` | Switch permission modes |
| `Ctrl+C` | Cancel current input |
| `Ctrl+L` | Clear the terminal screen |
| `/` | Open the command menu (at start of input) |

blocks.title: Give Claude Code a task and watch the tool calls. Try: 'Find the largest file in this directory and tell me what it contains.' Count the tool calls. For each one, write down whether it was gather, act, or verify.
blocks.solution: You should see Claude make at least 2-3 tool calls: one to list/search files (Bash or Glob — gather), one to read the largest file (Read — gather), then Claude synthesizes a response. If the file is very large, Claude might read only part of it. Every tool call should be visible in your terminal with the tool name shown.

questions: Did the permission prompts make you feel more or less comfortable letting Claude work? Why?
When Claude made tool calls, were you able to follow what it was doing? Or did it move too fast?
If you pressed Esc to stop Claude mid-action, what happened? This is an important safety control to practice.

blocks.content: Claude Code shows you everything it does through tool calls and permission prompts. Learning to read these signals — and knowing you can press Esc to stop at any time — is how you stay in control of the agentic loop.

blocks.prompt: You can now read what Claude Code is doing. The next module explains the invisible constraint behind everything: the context window. Understanding this single concept will prevent the most common frustration new users experience.
